title: Chapter 33: Vance's Lab
content: The files lined the wall in steel drawers. Each one labeled. Each one a life.
SUBJECT 01 — TERMINATED.
SUBJECT 02 — TERMINATED.
SUBJECT 03 — Alexander Chen. STATUS: ACTIVE / UNCONTAINED.

Wren's breath stopped. Subject 03 was Ash. His file. Right here, in a steel drawer, in a lab she was never supposed to see. Alexander Chen. Not just a name — a designation. A number. Three years of his existence reduced to a tab in a filing system.

She pulled the drawer open. Inside: a thick folder, paper and digital. His admission photo — a younger Ash, unlined, unbroken, staring at the camera with the hollow trust of someone who believed he was being helped. His medical history. His dosing schedule. His progression notes. Stage 1 onset: 48 hours. Stage 2 onset: 11 days. Stage 3 onset: 34 days. Notes in Vance's handwriting: *Candidate demonstrates exceptional neural plasticity. Recommend extended observation.*
Extended observation. That's what they called it when someone disappeared and you kept watching to see what happened next.
Wren closed the drawer. Her hands were trembling again — the buzzing rising, pressing against her skin. She forced it down. Focus. She needed to focus.
She moved along the wall.

SUBJECT 04 — TERMINATED.
SUBJECT 05 — CONTAINED.
SUBJECT 06 — TERMINATED.
SUBJECT 07 — MISSING.
SUBJECT 08 — TERMINATED.
SUBJECT 09 — CONTAINED.
SUBJECT 10 — CONTAINED.
SUBJECT 11 — MISSING.
SUBJECT 12 — TERMINATED.
Twelve subjects before her. Eight terminated. Two contained. Two missing. A mortality rate that would shut down any legitimate clinical trial. But this was never legitimate. This was never a trial.
And then:
SUBJECT 13 — Wren Ellison. STATUS: ACTIVE / UNCONTAINED.
She stared at her own name. Her own number. Thirteen.

The room tilted. Not physically — something inside her shifted, a tectonic plate of identity grinding against its neighbor. She'd been a patient. She'd walked into that clinic with anxiety and walked out with nothing — no face, no presence, no self. And Vance had known. From the very first pill, he'd known what it would do. He'd designed it to do exactly this.
The clinical trial was never a trial. She was never a patient.
She was a test subject.

Wren pulled her drawer open. The admission photo showed a woman she barely recognized. Dark hair, tired face, the kind of smile you give a camera when you're trying to look like you believe things will get better. Her dosing schedule was identical to Ash's. Her progression notes were clinical, detached, scientific.
*Subject 13 reports heightened auditory sensitivity consistent with Stage 2 onset. Recommend continued dosing at current levels.*
*Subject 13 demonstrates incipient electromagnetic anomaly. Stage 3 transition probable within 72 hours. Recommend acceleration of monitoring protocols.*
*Subject 13 has evaded containment. Location unknown. Recommend deployment of Tracking Hound prototype.*
They'd sent the hounds for her. Specifically. She wasn't an accidental loss — she was a data point that went off-grid. A result that needed to be recaptured and filed.

The nausea was back. Worse than the display case. This wasn't a marketing slogan — this was the architecture. The blueprint of her erasure, documented in Vance's neat handwriting, filed alphabetically with all the other damage.

Wren pulled the file from the drawer. All of it. Paper and drive. She grabbed Ash's file too. Then Subject 01, Subject 02, Subject 04, Subject 08 — the terminated ones, the ones who didn't survive to be horrified. Evidence. Proof.
Her hands were full and shaking and the buzzing in her palms was rising again, climbing, pressing against the skin like something trying to hatch.
The Signal Key sat on its pedestal in the center of the room. Pulsing. Patient. Waiting.
Wren took it.

The moment her fingers closed around the titanium frame, the pulse changed — quickened, aligned, syncing to something inside her that she hadn't known existed. A resonance. A frequency. The Key recognized her. Or she recognized it.
She didn't have time to wonder what that meant.
A sound from the corridor. Footsteps. Multiple sets, heavy and synchronized. Security.
They were coming.

Wren shoved the files into her bag. The Key went into her jacket — pressed against her ribs, still pulsing, still resonating with something in her blood. She moved to the door she'd forced open and pressed her back to the wall.
The footsteps grew louder. Closer.

She peeked around the doorframe. Four guards in the corridor, sweeping methodically, flashlights cutting through the sterile white. They hadn't reached Vance's door yet. They didn't know she was here.
But they would.

Wren reached out with her perception. The lights in the corridor — she could feel them. The cameras, the electronic locks, the temperature sensors in the containment units. A web of current and signal, all within her reach.
She took a breath. Held it.
Then she pulled.

Every light in the corridor died. The guards' flashlights snapped on, beams swinging wildly. The containment units lost power — the amber vials warming in their glass cases. An alarm tried to sound and failed, its speaker crackling into static.
Chaos. Four seconds of it.
Wren ran.

She burst from Vance's lab and barreled down the corridor, invisible and fast, her shoulder clipping one guard's arm as she passed. He spun, grabbing at nothing, his flashlight beam cutting across empty air.
The stairwell door was at the corridor's end. She hit it with her hip and it crashed open. Stairs — up. Four floors to the main building. Four floors of exposure.
She took them two at a time, her feet slapping concrete, her breath ragged, the Signal Key thumping against her ribs with every step.
Sublevel 1. Ground level. The stairwell door opened onto the lobby.
Alarms were screaming now. Real alarms — not the fire suppressions Ash had triggered, but security lockdowns. Red lights strobed. The lobby was a maze of movement — guards, staff, responders.
She was in the building. The building knew she was here.
And somewhere above her, Ash was waiting.
